course_description:
WRIT1200 introduces the fundamentals of creativity, writing and storytelling for communication professionals and media producers.

WRIT1200 is a core component of the first year of the Bachelor of Communication. The course is compulsory for both Strategic Communication and Digital Media majors. It is also a compulsory course for the Professional Writing and Communication major in the Bachelor of Arts.ﾠ

Students are introduced to fundamental principles and practices of writing, including creativity & design, reader engagement, storytelling, and the editing process.

Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email
timetabling.commarts@enquire.uq.edu.au
from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: This course is designed to introduce students to a range of concepts and practices fundamental to their development as communication professionals and to the production of creative and effective writing.

learning_outcomes.description: understand writing as a creative, problem-solving process
work with the elements of story and other strategies to produce engaging, persuasive, & reader-focused writing
design and produce a creative and well-written response to a project brief
understand the importance of regular writing, reading, and reflection for your development as a communication professional
analyse how written texts are constructed and evaluate their effectiveness
identify and address common writing problems in your own and others' work

assessment_details.title: In-class exercises
assessment_details.task_description: The tutorials in Weeks 3, 4, 5, 9, 10, 11, & 12 will involve a 20 minute worksheet with questions designed to test your ability to apply the concepts and techniques introduced in that week's lecture. Your best 5 results out of 7 will determine the mark out of 20. For further details on what is required and how it will be assessed, please refer to Blackboard. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Work is hand-written and submitted for assessment to tutors during class.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
There is no requirement that students complete all 7 in-class exercises but they are welcome to request access to a missed exercise (if they have a medical certification or equivalent to explain their absence) by contacting the course coordinator Nat (n.collie@uq.edu.au) as soon as possible after the missed tutorial.
Late submission
There is no requirement that students complete all 7 in-class exercises but they are welcome to request access to a missed exercise (if they have a medical certificate or equivalent to explain their absence) by contacting the course coordinator Nat (n.collie@uq.edu.au) as soon as possible after the missed tutorial.

assessment_details.title: Writing project, stage one: Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: The writing project - a short zine - involves two stages. Stage one involves presenting a project proposal in the Week 7 tutorial. Students will be required to discuss their zine design in small groups for feedback before submitting the proposal and a short reflection written during class time to their tutor for assessment by end of class. For further information on what is required and how it will be assessed, please refer to the Assessment page on Blackboard. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This assignment requires submission of the original hardcopy in your scheduled Week 7 tutorial. Digital submission (emailed or uploaded) is not available.
Active participation in class is also a requirement of this task. The final mark out of 15 will be capped at a pass (ie 7.5/15) if a student does not attend or actively participate in the group workshop activity.
If a student misses the tutorial and doesn't have grounds for an extension, they will need to email their tutor and course coordinator to organse a time to submit the work in-person by the end of Week 7 (ie 4pm Friday 17 April 2026). Any submission after this date will incur late penalties in addition to the cap of a pass because the student did not attend or participate in the group workshop activity.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If you are unwell or have another serious reason for not attending the tutorial (and have the necessary documentation eg medical certificate), you can submit an extension request via MyRequests.
If approved, email the course coordinator (n.collie@uq.edu.au) to organise in-person submission. If you wish to avoid the mark on this assignment being capped at a pass, you will need to organise sufficient time with the coordinator to discuss your work, receive feedback, and write a short reflection ie about 20 minutes.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
If a student misses the tutorial and doesn't have grounds for an extension, they will need to email their tutor and course coordinator to organse a time to submit the work in-person by the end of Week 7 (ie 4pm Friday 17 April 2026). Any submission after this date will incur late penalties in addition to the cap of a pass because they did not attend or participate in the group workshop activity.

assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If an extension is approved and you wish to submit a hardcopy in addition to the digital submission (and thus avoid having the mark capped at a pass), please contact the course coordinator (n.collie@uq.edu.au) to organise in-person submission.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Late submissions require hardcopy submission in addition to digitial submission to avoid the mark being capped at a pass. Please contact the course coordinator (n.collie@uq.edu.au) to organise in-person submission.

assessment_details.title: Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The end of semester exam will use a combination of multiple choice and short answer questions to assess the ability of students to apply key concepts from the course. Please refer to the Assessment page on Blackboard for further details and example questions. We will also discuss how to prepare for the exam in tutorials. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

The dictionary must be free of any hand-written notes or annotations. Exam platform Inspera Invigilation Invigilated in person

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Writing, reading, and creativity No tutorials this week Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 2: Engaging your reader: Context, medium, & format
Week 2: Getting started
Week 3: Engaging your reader: POV, voice, & tone
Week 3: Writing for your reader
Week 4: Engaging your reader: Persuasion
Week 4: Persuading your reader
Week 5: Writing stories: People, places, problems
Week 5: Developing your project proposal
Week 6: Writing stories: Visual storytelling No tutorials this week. Use the free time to work on your zine project proposal (due after the mid-semester break).
MID SEMESTER BREAK - No lectures or tutorials
Week 7: Proposal workshop No lecture this week. Zine project proposals are due in tutorials this week.
Week 8: Writing well: The drafting and revision process Learning outcomes: L01
Week 9: Writing well: Show, don't (just) tell Learning outcomes: L01, L05, L06
Week 9: Narrating your story
Week 10: Writing well: Structure
Week 10: Working on your structure and layout Learning outcomes: L04
Week 11: Writing well: Editing and style issues Learning outcomes: L01, L05, L06
Week 11: Editing Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06
Week 12: Writing well: Proofreading and correcting errors Learning outcomes: L01, L05
Week 12: Proofreading Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06

Week 13: Zine submission and consultation period In addition to submitting the hardcopy of their zine project, students can also use this final tutorial to catch up on any missed worksheets (if late completion has been approved by the course coordinator) and consult with tutors regarding preparation for the final exam. Learning outcomes: L03, L05, L06

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
